summary: Gruber dismantles Qualcomm's claims that its forthcoming Snapdragon X Elite processor will beat Apple's M2 chip, pointing out the comparison is deeply misleading. The X Elite won't ship until mid-2024 at the earliest, yet is being compared to a chip Apple debuted in June 2022. Qualcomm only touts peak multi-core performance with 50% more cores and higher power draw, while conspicuously omitting single-core numbers. Gruber argues the deeper problem is that ARM on Windows has never gained traction because Microsoft won't commit to it, and Apple silicon's advantage comes from tight hardware-software integration that Qualcomm can't replicate with Windows. He notes Qualcomm's phone chips have always lagged years behind Apple's A-series, making claims of PC chip superiority implausible.
key_points: Qualcomm is comparing unreleased mid-2024 chips against Apple's M2, which debuted two full years earlier in June 2022
The comparison cherry-picks peak multi-core performance using 50% more cores and higher power draw, while likely hiding inferior single-core performance
ARM-based PCs represent less than 1% of PC sales, and Microsoft has shown no willingness to go all-in on ARM with Windows
Apple silicon's advantage stems from tight vertical integration of hardware and software that Qualcomm cannot replicate through a partnership with Microsoft
Qualcomm's track record of missed deadlines and phone chips lagging years behind Apple's A-series undermines their PC chip claims
money_quotes: That's like a professional sprinter bragging about winning the 100-meter dash against a bunch of marathon champions.
If Mac hardware and MacOS were made by separate companies, and the MacOS software company licensed their OS to other OEMs, I really don't think Apple silicon hardware would have happened.

The seemingly too-good-to-be-true performance of Apple silicon Macs is the result of the silicon being designed for the software and the software being optimized and at very low levels designed for the silicon.
Qualcomm isn't going to get that from Microsoft with Windows.

The idea that they're even vaguely catching up to Apple silicon is laughable, and it's frustrating that so much of the tech press took anything Qualcomm claimed about relative performance against Apple silicon seriously.

We know for a fact that their Snapdragon chips for phones have always lagged years behind Apple's A-series chips in both sheer performance and performance-per-watt, with no sign that they're catching up.
So how in the world would their ARM chips for PCs beat Apple's M-series chips?
key_insight: Qualcomm's performance claims against Apple silicon are misleading cherry-picks that ignore Apple's vertical integration advantage, and the tech press should know better than to take them seriously given Qualcomm's consistent track record of overpromising and underdelivering.
